name: Aegir
description: Aegir is the Norse god of the sea, known for his hospitality and brewing skills. Unlike some other deities, Aegir isn't directly associated with controlling the weather or navigation, but rather represents the powerful, often unpredictable, nature of the ocean depths. He resides in a magnificent hall beneath the waves, illuminated not by fire, but by glowing gold. Aegir hosts lavish feasts for the gods, brewing ale in a massive cauldron gifted to him. He is often depicted as an old man with long white hair and beard, representing the foam of the sea, and his demeanor is said to be both generous and fearsome, mirroring the dual nature of the ocean itself. He is married to Ran, a goddess who captures drowned sailors in her net, and together they have nine daughters, the waves themselves, often depicted as powerful and independent women. Aegir's feasts are a reminder of the sea's bounty, but also its inherent dangers, and his role is crucial in maintaining the balance between the gods and the unpredictable forces of nature. He is a powerful, ancient entity respected and feared by the Norse pantheon, embodying the vastness and mystery of the ocean's realm.
subtitle: God of the Sea and Brewing

keyMyths.title: Aegir's Feast
keyMyths.description: Aegir hosts lavish feasts for the gods, brewing ale in a giant cauldron. This highlights his hospitality and connection to brewing.

keyMyths.title: Kidnapping of the Mead
keyMyths.description: The gods obtain the Mead of Poetry from giants after Aegir's hall is used to store it. This demonstrates his connection to valuable objects and the gods' dealings.

keyMyths.title: Aegir's Daughters
keyMyths.description: Aegir's nine daughters, the waves, are described and their characteristics associated with different types of waves. This showcases the connection between Aegir and the sea's physical manifestations.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, containing accounts of Aegir and his family.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, containing accounts of Aegir and his family.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems that provide insights into Norse beliefs and mythology, including references to Aegir.
